sections.header: Verses 1–16 — "Greet One Another with a Holy Kiss"

heb.paragraph: Phoebe is described as a diakonos of the church at Cenchreae (v. 1). The masculine form of the noun applied to a woman is significant: Paul uses the same term for himself (1 Cor 3:5) and for Apollos (1 Cor 3:5). Whether this indicates a formal office (‘deacon’) or a functional description (‘servant’) is debated, but Paul also calls Phoebe a prostatis (‘patron’ / ‘benefactor’, v. 2), indicating social prominence and financial support of Paul’s mission.

In v. 7 Paul describes Andronicus and Junia as ‘outstanding among the apostles’ (episēmoi en tois apostolois). The identification of Junia as a woman and as an apostle has been extensively debated. The name Iounian is almost certainly feminine (Junia), not a contraction of the masculine Junianus. The phrase may mean ‘well known among the apostles’ (they were recognised by the apostles) or ‘notable as apostles’ (they themselves were apostles in the broader sense of ‘missionaries’).

panels.ctx: Romans 16:1–16 contains the longest greeting list in any Pauline letter: 26 individuals and several households are named. This is remarkable for a church Paul had not founded or visited, and it reveals the extensive network of relationships Paul had built across the Mediterranean. The list includes Jews and Gentiles, men and women, slaves and free persons, reflecting the social diversity of the Roman house churches. Phoebe of Cenchreae (vv. 1–2) is commended as the letter’s carrier, a common practice in ancient letter-writing. The greeting list functions both as personal connection and as a strategic move: naming allies establishes Paul’s credibility with a church he has not yet visited.

cross.note: Priscilla and Aquila, tentmakers who worked with Paul in Corinth and later hosted a house church.
A parallel greeting list at the end of Colossians, showing the same pattern.
Greetings to Apphia, Archippus, and the church in Philemon’s house — the house church model.

notes.note: Phoebe is commended as a servant of the church and a patron (prostatis) of many, including Paul himself. She is almost certainly the carrier of the letter and its first public reader.
Priscilla and Aquila risked their lives for Paul and host a house church. The fact that Priscilla is named first in four of six NT references suggests her prominence in ministry.
Andronicus and Junia were in Christ before Paul and are described as prominent among the apostles. This is one of the earliest Christian couples known to us.
The ‘holy kiss’ was the standard liturgical greeting of the early church, expressing the familial bond of believers as brothers and sisters in Christ.
Calvin acknowledges Phoebe’s ministry and notes that women served the church in significant roles from the earliest period. The term diakonos denotes genuine service, not mere honorary title.
Calvin praises Priscilla and Aquila as examples of Christian marriage devoted to the gospel. Their willingness to risk their lives for Paul demonstrates the radical commitment the gospel produces.

The term prostatis (‘patron’ / ‘benefactor’) in v. 2 denotes a person of means who provides financial and social support. In the Greco-Roman patronage system, this was a position of considerable social standing.

The name Iounian is accusative and could be either Iounian (feminine, Junia) or Iounian (masculine, Junias). The overwhelming weight of patristic evidence and manuscript analysis supports the feminine Junia.

Moo notes that the greeting list reveals the social composition of the early Roman church: a mixture of Jewish and Gentile Christians, several of whom Paul knew from other cities. The diversity of names (Latin, Greek, and Jewish) reflects the cosmopolitan character of Rome itself.

Schreiner argues that Phoebe’s role as letter-carrier was significant: she would have been the first to read the letter publicly and would have been expected to explain and interpret it. Her commendation is Paul’s way of authorising her role.

sections.header: Verses 17–27 — "To the Only Wise God Be Glory Forever"

heb.paragraph: Mystērion in v. 25 echoes 11:25: the ‘mystery hidden for long ages past’ is now disclosed through the prophetic writings and made known to all nations. The mystery is the inclusion of Gentiles alongside Jews in the one people of God through faith in Christ. The doxology (vv. 25–27) bookends the letter: what began with the gospel ‘promised beforehand through his prophets’ (1:2) now concludes with the mystery ‘revealed and made known through the prophetic writings.’

panels.ctx: Romans 16:17–27 contains Paul’s final warning against divisive teachers (vv. 17–20) and the letter’s concluding doxology (vv. 25–27). The warning is brief but sharp: those who cause divisions serve their own appetites and deceive the naive with smooth talk. The promise that ‘the God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet’ (v. 20) is a dramatic allusion to Genesis 3:15, casting the church’s conflict with false teaching as the cosmic battle between the seed of the woman and the serpent. The doxology is one of the most magnificent in the NT, summarising the entire letter’s argument: the gospel is the revelation of a mystery kept secret for ages but now made known for the obedience of faith among all nations.

cross.note: The proto-evangelium: God will crush the serpent’s head — the OT background for v. 20.
The mystery of Christ: Gentiles are fellow heirs with Israel — the same mystery Paul references.
A parallel doxology: to the only God our Saviour be glory, majesty, power, and authority.

notes.note: Paul warns against those who cause divisions and put obstacles in the believers’ path. These people serve their own appetites, not Christ, and use smooth talk to deceive the unsuspecting.

The God of peace will crush Satan under your feet. This is a remarkable promise: the cosmic victory of Genesis 3:15 is applied to the church’s present struggle. Satan’s defeat is certain and imminent.

The doxology recapitulates the letter: the gospel is a mystery now revealed, proclaimed to all nations for the obedience of faith. The letter began with the ‘obedience of faith’ (1:5) and ends with it (16:26).
Calvin warns that vigilance against false teachers is a permanent duty of the church. Smooth speech and flattery are the tools of deception; sound doctrine is the antidote.

Calvin sees the doxology as the fitting conclusion: all theology must end in worship. The mystery now revealed is the inclusion of the nations in God’s covenant through Christ, and the only appropriate response is ascribing glory to God forever.

The phrase ‘crush Satan under your feet’ (syntribo ton satanan hypo tous podas) combines Genesis 3:15 imagery with the language of military triumph. The church participates in Christ’s victory over the ancient adversary.

The textual placement of the doxology varies among manuscripts: some place it after 14:23, others after 15:33, and some omit it entirely. The majority of scholars accept its authenticity and its position at the end of chapter 16.

Moo reads the doxology as an intentional inclusio with 1:1–7. The letter opens with the gospel promised through the prophets and closes with the mystery revealed through the prophetic writings. The ‘obedience of faith’ phrase in both 1:5 and 16:26 creates a perfect ring structure around the entire letter.

Schreiner argues that the doxology’s emphasis on God’s wisdom (mono sophō theō, ‘the only wise God’) recalls the doxology of 11:33–36 and summarises the entire letter’s argument. The mystery of salvation is the supreme display of divine wisdom, and the only appropriate response is eternal praise.

rows.label: vv.25–27
rows.text: Doxology: To the Only Wise God

lit.note: Romans 16 closes the letter with personal greetings (vv. 1–16), a warning against false teachers (vv. 17–20), companion greetings (vv. 21–24), and the great doxology (vv. 25–27) that forms an inclusio with 1:1–7.

themes.note: Mission at 8 reflects the greeting network and the mystery revealed to all nations. Worship at 7 captures the closing doxology. Faith at 7 reflects the inclusio with ‘obedience of faith’ from 1:5.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: Andronicus and Junia were in Christ before Paul and are described as prominent among the apostles…

ppl.name: Phoebe
ppl.text: Phoebe is commended as a servant of the church and a patron (prostatis) of many, including Paul himself…

ppl.name: Priscilla
ppl.role: Teacher and co-worker with Paul and Apollos
ppl.text: Priscilla (Prisca) and her husband Aquila are Jewish tentmakers expelled from Rome under Claudius. They meet Paul at Corinth (Acts 18:2) and join his team as both co-workers in tentmaking and teachers of the faith. When Apollos arrives at Ephesus with incomplete knowledge, Priscilla and Aquila "took him aside and explained to him the way of God more adequately" (Acts 18:26). She is mentioned before Aquila four of six times in the NT, suggesting her prominence.

ppl.name: Aquila
ppl.role: Tentmaker and co-worker; husband of Priscilla
ppl.text: Aquila is a Jewish tentmaker from Pontus who with Priscilla becomes one of Paul’s most important co-workers. The couple host a church in their home in both Ephesus and Rome (1 Cor 16:19; Rom 16:3–5) and risk their lives for Paul (Rom 16:4). Their partnership with Paul in tentmaking while he is at Corinth models the early church’s pattern of self-supporting ministry.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Greetings and Final Instructions

translations.text: Greet also the church that meets at their house. Greet my dear friend Epenetus, who was the first convert to Christ in the province of Asia.
Greet also the church in their house. Greet my beloved Epaenetus, who was the first convert to Christ in Asia.
They risked their lives for me. Not only I but all the churches of the Gentiles are grateful to them.
who risked their necks for my life, to whom not only I give thanks but all the churches of the Gentiles give thanks as well.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Phoebe is commended as a servant of the church and a patron (prostatis) of many, including Paul himself. She is almost certainly the carrier of the letter and its first public reader."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "Calvin acknowledges Phoebe’s ministry and notes that women served the church in significant roles from the earliest period. The term diakonos denotes genuine service, not mere honorary title."

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The term prostatis (‘patron’ / ‘benefactor’) in v. 2 denotes a person of means who provides financial and social support. In the Greco-Roman patronage system, this was a position of considerable socia…"

debate: The textual placement of the doxology varies among manuscripts: some place it after 14:23, others after 15:33, and some omit it entirely. The majority…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Greetings and Final Instructions within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Priscilla and Aquila, tentmakers who worked with Paul in Corinth and later hosted a house church.
A parallel greeting list at the end of Colossians, showing the same pattern.
Greetings to Apphia, Archippus, and the church in Philemon’s house — the house church model.
The proto-evangelium: God will crush the serpent’s head — the OT background for v. 20.
The mystery of Christ: Gentiles are fellow heirs with Israel — the same mystery Paul references.
A parallel doxology: to the only God our Saviour be glory, majesty, power, and authority.

discourse.thesis: Paul commends Phoebe, greets the Roman believers, warns against divisive teachers, and closes with a doxology to the God who can establish them.

nodes.text: Paul commends Phoebe, a servant (διάκονον) of the church at Cenchreae. Welcome her in the Lord in a manner worthy of the saints, and help her in whatever she needs — she has been a patron of many, including Paul himself.

Greetings to Prisca and Aquila, Paul's fellow workers who risked their necks for his life — all the churches of the Gentiles give thanks for them. Greet the church in their house. Greet Epaenetus, the first convert in Asia. Greet Mary, who worked hard for you. Greet Andronicus and Junia, kinsmen and fellow prisoners, outstanding among the apostles, in Christ before Paul. [Paul names 26 individuals and groups.] Greet one another with a holy kiss. All the churches of Christ greet you.

Watch out for those who cause divisions and create obstacles contrary to the doctrine you have been taught — avoid them. Such people do not serve our Lord Christ but their own appetites, deceiving the hearts of the naive by smooth talk and flattery. Your obedience is known to all; Paul rejoices over them but wants them to be wise as to what is good and innocent as to what is evil. The God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet. The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you.

Timothy, Paul's fellow worker, sends greetings. So do Lucius, Jason, and Sosipater, Paul's kinsmen. Tertius, who wrote this letter, greets them in the Lord. Gaius, host to Paul and the whole church, greets them. Erastus the city treasurer and brother Quartus send greetings.

Now to him who is able to strengthen you according to Paul's gospel and the preaching of Jesus Christ, according to the revelation of the mystery hidden for long ages but now disclosed and made known through the prophetic writings — according to the command of the eternal God — to bring about the obedience of faith among all the nations: to the only wise God be glory forevermore through Jesus Christ! Amen.

discourse.note: Romans 16 is the most extensive list of greetings in Paul's letters, including 26 individuals. Phoebe likely carried the letter to Rome. The warning against divisive teachers (vv. 17-20) is unexpectedly sharp. The final doxology (vv. 25-27) echoes 1:5 ('obedience of faith') and 11:33-36, framing the entire letter.
